stories.headline: One killed, one injured in Aurora crash involving stolen motorcycle; driver flees scene
stories.summary:
A late-night crash in Aurora left one man dead and a woman hospitalized after the motorcycle they were riding was struck by a turning vehicle near East Iliff Avenue and South Idalia Street at approximately 11:49 p.m. Saturday. The motorcycle had been reported stolen, and both riders were transported to the hospital, where the man later died. His identity is being withheld pending notification by the Arapahoe County Coroner's Office.

The striking vehicle, a Honda CR-V, had turned left onto South Idalia Street when it hit the motorcycle traveling westbound on East Iliff. The CR-V driver fled the scene, and investigators found the car abandoned a short distance away. No suspects have been publicly identified or arrested as of Sunday morning.

Aurora police said speed may have been a factor in the crash. The investigation remains ongoing.

stories.headline: Pedestrian killed after being struck by RTD A Line commuter rail train in Denver
stories.summary:
One person was killed Saturday afternoon after being struck by a Regional Transportation District A Line commuter rail train in Denver, according to Denver police. The incident occurred around 1:15 p.m., prompting RTD to replace A Line service with shuttle buses for a period following the crash. Minimal details were released by authorities.

Denver police confirmed the fatality but did not immediately release the identity of the victim or details about the circumstances leading to the collision. RTD has not issued a formal statement detailing the cause of the incident.

The A Line connects Denver Union Station to Denver International Airport. Service disruptions affected passengers during the Saturday afternoon period while emergency responders were on scene.

stories.headline: Rep. Diana DeGette faces first serious primary challenge in decades from two younger Denver Democrats
stories.summary:
U.S. Rep. Diana DeGette, who has represented Colorado's 1st Congressional District since 1997, is facing her most competitive Democratic primary in years as she seeks a 16th term. Two challengers — attorney and barista Melat Kiros, 29, and University of Colorado Regent Wanda James, 62 — are arguing that DeGette's three decades in Congress have not translated into effective leadership for Denver constituents.

Kiros dealt DeGette a symbolic blow earlier this year by soundly defeating her at the Denver County Democratic assembly — the first time DeGette had lost such a vote since her initial election. DeGette narrowly cleared the threshold to appear on the June 30 ballot at the congressional district assembly, while Kiros earned top billing and James qualified via petition.

DeGette argues her seniority positions her to lead a powerful House subcommittee if Democrats retake the majority, potentially advancing Medicare for All legislation and abortion access protections. Her opponents counter that seniority without results is insufficient, and former Denver Mayor Wellington Webb, who has endorsed James, said DeGette has 'lost some contact with her constituents.' The winner of the Democratic primary will be the heavy favorite in November against presumptive Republican nominee Christy Peterson in the deeply Democratic district.

stories.headline: Target opens largest food distribution center yet in Thornton, employing 380 workers
stories.summary:
Target has opened its largest fresh food distribution center to date in Thornton, a facility covering more than half a million square feet of refrigerated space that will serve an 11-state region. The center employs 380 workers, including 37 in salaried positions, with hourly wages ranging from $15 to $24. Most of the facility is maintained at 34 degrees, with freezer sections reaching minus 15 degrees.

The Thornton facility is part of Target's broader effort to build its own refrigerated distribution network after years of relying on third-party vendors, a strategy that added cost and reduced supply chain efficiency. Senior Vice President Amy Probst said the facility will significantly reduce the time fresh food sits in transit before reaching stores and will allow local products — such as Palisade peaches — to be distributed without unnecessary cross-country routing.

Target currently employs approximately 9,300 people across 45 Colorado stores, along with workers at a dry goods distribution facility in Pueblo and a sorting center in Denver. The Thornton hub is the company's third new refrigerated distribution center built in four years as it works to close a competitive gap with Walmart and Kroger in the grocery segment.

stories.headline: Rapids defender Lucas Herrington, 18, named to Australia's World Cup roster as only teenager in squad
stories.summary:
Colorado Rapids center back Lucas Herrington has been named to Australia's official 26-man World Cup roster, becoming the only Rapids player called up to a World Cup squad ahead of Monday's selection deadline. At 18 years old, Herrington is the youngest player on the Socceroos' roster. He most recently started and played 89 minutes in Australia's 1-0 friendly loss to Mexico on Saturday.

Herrington joined the Rapids this past offseason after signing a pre-contract midway through the 2025 MLS season. He has started all 15 MLS matches this season, playing every minute, and has earned three MLS Team of the Matchday honors and scored a goal in his Dick's Sporting Goods Park debut against Portland. Analytics platform American Soccer Analysis ranks him as a top-10 passer and top-2 dribbler among MLS center backs by goals added.

Australia is placed in World Cup Group D alongside host nation the United States, Turkiye, and Paraguay. The Socceroos open group play against Turkiye on June 13 and face the United States on June 19. Herrington's World Cup participation will require him to miss Rapids matches during that stretch.

stories.headline: Broncos linebacker Alex Singleton declares Denver 'the target now' in the AFC ahead of 2026 season
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos captain and inside linebacker Alex Singleton spoke to reporters Friday at his second annual charity cornhole tournament benefiting Special Olympics Colorado, expressing confidence that the team has positioned itself as a top contender in the AFC heading into the 2026 season. Singleton, who signed a new contract in March worth $11 million in guaranteed money, said the Broncos' offseason philosophy of retaining proven veterans rather than overhauling the roster sets the team apart.

Singleton and fellow inside linebacker Justin Strnad both re-signed with Denver after testing free agency, with Strnad telling reporters he was not nervous about entering the market. The Broncos cut 2025 signee Dre Greenlaw as part of the moves. Head coach Sean Payton has emphasized continuity, and Singleton said the organization has followed through on its stated intentions.

Beyond the linebacker room, the Broncos made a high-profile offseason acquisition by trading for Miami Dolphins wide receiver Jaylen Waddle. Singleton said the team's goal is no longer to chase the league's elite teams but to be the team others are aiming to beat. 'We're not chasing targets anymore,' Singleton said. 'We're the target, now, I think, in the AFC.'

stories.headline: DU hockey goaltender Johnny Hicks named Pioneers' Male Athlete of the Year after record-setting national title run
stories.summary:
University of Denver freshman goaltender Johnny Hicks has been named Denver Athletics' Male Student-Athlete of the Year after leading the Pioneers to their 11th NCAA hockey championship this spring. Hicks was named the tournament's Most Outstanding Player and set a school record with a 16-0-1 record and a 1.19 goals-against average. His .957 save percentage set an NCAA record.

Denver defeated Wisconsin 2-1 on April 11 to claim the national title. Hicks's performance throughout the season and tournament drew widespread recognition as one of the most dominant single-season efforts by a college goaltender in recent memory.

The Male Athlete of the Year award caps a remarkable debut collegiate season for Hicks, who arrived at DU as a freshman and immediately became the centerpiece of one of the program's most successful campaigns.

stories.headline: Boulder County extends direct cash assistance program for low-income parents, doubles monthly payments to $600
stories.summary:
Boulder County commissioners have approved approximately $4.2 million to extend the county's Nurturing Futures direct cash assistance pilot program for an additional 15 months, through December, with a second round expected to begin in January. The current program, which provides $300 per month to qualifying low-income parents of young children, was originally set to expire in September. Under the extension, monthly payments will double to $600 starting in January to account for rising costs of living.

The original two-year pilot launched in July 2024 and has served more than 541 families, who are free to use the funds for any household need including groceries, rent, childcare, and medical expenses. Participants must have at least one child under age 4, earn 30% or less of the area median income, and reside in Boulder County. Funding for the extension comes from interest earned on the county's American Rescue Plan Act allocation.

County Commissioner Marta Loachamin said the program is designed to give parents the flexibility to address their household's most pressing needs. Boulder County officials estimate at least 250 families will continue to qualify for assistance under the extension.

stories.headline: Colorado colleges brace for enrollment cliff as high school graduate numbers begin long-term decline
stories.summary:
Colorado higher education institutions are confronting a demographic challenge as the number of high school graduates in the state is projected to decline 12% between 2023 and 2041, according to the Western Interstate Commission for Higher Education. The trend stems from a 16% nationwide drop in U.S. births between 2007 and 2025 — an 8% decline in Colorado during the same period — driven largely by reduced pregnancies among teens and young women and economic instability during the Great Recession.

Nationally, high school graduations peaked in 2025 at nearly 3.9 million and are expected to fall steadily through 2041. Colorado graduated 60,387 students in the 2024-25 school year. Many state institutions have managed to maintain enrollment growth so far, but higher education officials say the steeper part of the decline is approaching.

Adams State University in the San Luis Valley reported a 4.7% enrollment increase for fall 2025 after roughly 15 years of decline, but its president cautioned that competition among colleges will intensify as the pool of traditional-age applicants shrinks. Experts warn that the trend could affect the supply of degree-required workers in critical fields such as nursing and education, while institutions face financial pressure from reduced tuition revenue.

stories.headline: Denver Zoo welcomes baby Sumatran orangutan, invites public to vote on name
stories.summary:
The Denver Zoo has welcomed a male Sumatran orangutan born May 24 to first-time mother Hesty, who herself was born at the zoo in 2010. The birth followed a seven-hour labor, and zoo officials report that Hesty has demonstrated strong maternal instincts. Assistant Curator Molly Kainuma, who helped care for Hesty as an infant, called the birth a full-circle moment.

The Denver Zoo is inviting the public to help name the newborn by voting among three options — Rambutan, Oka, or Jamartin — for a $5 donation per vote, with proceeds supporting conservation efforts. Rambutan is named after a tropical fruit native to Southeast Asia; Oka is a traditional Balinese name meaning 'child' or 'heir'; Jamartin honors Indonesian orangutan conservationist Dr. Jamartin Sihite.

Visitors may catch limited glimpses of the mother and infant in coming weeks, though the Great Apes building may be temporarily closed at times. The birth is considered significant for conservation of Sumatran orangutans, a critically endangered species, through the Association of Zoos and Aquariums' Species Survival Plan.

stories.headline: Climber airlifted from Clear Creek Canyon after accident near Highway 6
stories.summary:
A climber was airlifted from Clear Creek Canyon on Saturday afternoon following an accident along the popular climbing corridor near the intersection of Highway 40 and Highway 6 in Clear Creek County. A Flight for Life helicopter was staged at the scene, and Highway 6 was temporarily closed to allow for the emergency response.

The Clear Creek County Sheriff's Office confirmed the patient had been airlifted and that all emergency responders had cleared the scene by approximately 3:45 p.m. The condition of the injured climber was not disclosed. Clear Creek EMS, Alpine Rescue Team, and the Clear Creek Fire Authority all responded to the incident.

Clear Creek Canyon is a well-known climbing destination located roughly 30 miles west of Denver, drawing climbers from across the Front Range. The cause of the accident was not detailed in official statements.

stories.headline: Morgan Wallen flips piano on stage at Empower Field during Denver concert after technical difficulties
stories.summary:
Country music star Morgan Wallen went viral after videos circulated showing him flipping a piano during his Friday night concert at Empower Field at Mile High in Denver. Wallen was mid-performance on the song 'Sand in My Boots' when he stood up from the instrument, told the crowd 'Just sing it a cappella, this piano ain't working,' finished the song unaccompanied, and then pushed the piano over. The moment drew mixed reactions on social media.

Wallen returned for a second Denver show Saturday with a replacement piano and acknowledged the previous night's incident to the crowd. The concerts were part of his Still the Problem Tour, an extension of his 2025 I'm the Problem Tour. Wallen is one of the best-selling country artists in recent years, with his 2023 album 'One Thing at a Time' breaking Garth Brooks' record for longest-running No. 1 country album.

The incident adds to a series of publicized off-stage controversies for Wallen, who in December 2024 pleaded guilty to two misdemeanor counts of reckless endangerment after throwing a chair off a Nashville bar roof in 2024, nearly striking two police officers. He was sentenced to seven days in a DUI education center and two years of supervised probation.

joke.joke: If my wife asks me what I want for dinner one more time, I'm going to open a restaurant called 'I Don't Care, Whatever You Want' and we can just eat there.

topStory.headline: U.S. Strikes Iranian Radar and Drone Sites; Iran Retaliates, Kuwait Hit by Missiles and Drones
topStory.summary:
The United States military carried out strikes on Iranian radar and drone control sites on Saturday and Sunday near the city of Goruk and on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz, saying the action was in response to Iran shooting down an American MQ-1 drone operating over international waters. U.S. Central Command said fighter aircraft eliminated Iranian air defenses, a ground control station and two one-way attack drones it described as posing clear threats to ships in regional waters, and reported no American personnel were harmed.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps acknowledged a retaliatory strike, saying its aerospace force targeted an air base it claimed the U.S. used to attack a telecommunications tower on Sirik Island in Hormozgan province. The IRGC warned that if U.S. attacks continued, its response would be "completely different" and that Washington would bear responsibility for the consequences. Iran's foreign ministry spokesman accused the U.S. of violating the ceasefire and said American negotiators were "constantly changing their views and putting forward new or contradictory demands."

Kuwait reported that its air defense systems intercepted incoming drone and missile fire early Monday morning, with state media saying air raid sirens sounded across the country. Kuwait's foreign ministry later issued a statement condemning what it called "heinous and repeated Iranian attacks" and described the strikes as a dangerous escalation. Kuwait is home to U.S. Army Central, the Army's Mideast forward command, and hosts U.S. military installations that have been targeted previously by Iran and its proxies.

The exchanges represent the latest breakdown in a nominal ceasefire agreed in early April, which has been repeatedly violated by both sides even as diplomatic talks on a broader settlement continue. Negotiations have stalled over disagreements on the future of Iran's nuclear and missile programs, the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz — which Iran has effectively closed to shipping, cutting off roughly one-fifth of global oil supply — and the removal of highly enriched uranium. CBS News reported that President Trump had recently requested changes to a draft deal related to those issues.

Trump addressed the tensions in an early Monday post on Truth Social, urging critics to "sit back and relax" and saying Iran "really wants to make a deal." Iran's chief negotiator has said Tehran will not agree to any terms unless Iranian rights are fully secured, and the country's foreign ministry has accused the U.S. of stalling with excessive demands. The war, which the U.S. and Israel launched on Feb. 28, has killed thousands of people, mostly in Iran and Lebanon, and has claimed the lives of 13 U.S. service members, while driving global energy prices sharply higher.
